original_abstract: Understanding the atomic-level structure of electrocatalysts under operating conditions is essential for identifying the mechanisms that drive their performance. In this study, we employ in situ X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) to investigate the structural evolution of Pt3Ni nanoframes, a class of highly efficient catalysts for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). By utilizing electrochemical hydrogen underpotential deposition and carbon monoxide electrooxidation, we demonstrate that nanoframes with distinct surface properties exhibit varying levels of binding strength to reaction intermediates. While the formation of a "Pt-skin" is known to optimize ORR activity by modulating surface binding energies, our results show that this process is highly dependent on initial platinum segregation. Specifically, nanoframes with lower activity possess rougher surfaces and higher Ni content near the surface, leading to deleterious Ni dissolution. In contrast, high-performance nanoframes exhibit a more pronounced segregation of platinum over a nickel-rich subsurface, facilitating the formation of a stable, high-quality Pt-skin. These findings highlight that the exceptional catalytic activity of Pt3Ni nanoframes originates from the successful atomic-level engineering of the Pt-skin structure, providing critical insights for the rational design of next-generation fuel cell catalysts.
humanized_abstract: Elucidating the atomic structure of electrocatalysts under working conditions is a major unsolved challenge in the field of catalysis and a prerequisite for establishing a consensus on their operating mechanism(s). Here, using in situ X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS), we for the first time detail the structural changes occurring at the atomic level in Pt3Ni nanoframes catalysts upon application as highly efficient oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) catalysts. These nanoframe catalysts, along with other synergistic structures, can be modified by a number of surface properties depending on how the hydrogens from the electrochemical hydrogen underpotential deposition (Hupd) and carbon monoxide to the carbon catalyst are attached. Moreover, the CO electrooxidation gives additional insight into surface and catalyst structures; nanoframes with differing surface characteristics vary in the degree of binding to the various reaction intermediate species adsorbed to the catalyst surface. It is well-established that maximizing the ORR performance via adjustment of the surface binding energies via the formation of a so-called “Pt-skin” structure largely relies on the initial stage of the platinum segregation, which is in part validated in this report. Moreover, there is a considerable relationship between the surface properties of the less active catalyst and the deleterious Ni-dissolution; nanoframes with higher Ni content, lower number of cycles as well as higher width of the surfaces have resulted in Ni-dissolution under working conditions. In contrary, those nanoframes with high activity (i.e., high number of cycles, smooth surfaces) show large differences of Pt-segregation over the Ni-rich subsurface, allowing thereby successful formation of stable and optimal quality Pt-skin structures. Thus, the ultimate atomic-level optimization in these highly efficient nanoalloy structure may well be reached in this report, thereby providing a highly insightful value to the designing of ultra-efficient new fuel cell catalysts.
